posts.title: AI agents went from demo to deploy
posts.hook:
Six months ago I watched an AI agent demo fill out a form and everyone clapped.

Last week I watched one enrich 400 accounts, score them, build sequences, and push to Instantly. Nobody clapped because it just... runs now.
posts.body:
Six months ago I watched an AI agent demo fill out a form and everyone clapped.

Last week I watched one enrich 400 accounts, score them, build sequences, and push to Instantly. Nobody clapped because it just... runs now.

The shift happened quietly. Frameworks like LangChain got stable enough to trust. Error handling got real. State management stopped being an afterthought.

But the teams shipping production agents right now share three things:

1. They treat agents like infrastructure, not features. Logging, retries, fallbacks. The boring stuff.
2. They scope ruthlessly. One agent does one job well before it touches a second.
3. They have someone who understands both the code and the workflow it replaces.

That third one is the bottleneck. Most AI agent failures I see aren't technical. They're workflow translation failures. The developer doesn't know what the SDR actually does at step 4, so the agent hallucinates a shortcut.

The teams winning aren't the ones with the best models. They're the ones where the person building the agent used to do the job manually.
posts.cta: If you're deploying agents into GTM workflows, what broke first... the tech or the process translation?

remixes.label: invisible-infrastructure
remixes.hook: The most important AI deployments are the ones nobody notices.
remixes.body:
The most important AI deployments are the ones nobody notices.

We got obsessed with demos. An agent fills out a form on stage, the crowd loses it. But demos are theater. Production is plumbing.

I run an ABM pipeline that enriches hundreds of accounts every night while I sleep. It scores them against ICP fit, builds personalized sequences, and pushes everything into outbound tools. No Zapier. No manual triggers. Just a cron job and a Python script with real error handling.

Nobody is impressed by this because there is nothing to see. No chat interface. No streaming text. Just a JSON file that is fuller in the morning than it was at night.

That is the actual state of AI agents in 2026. The good ones disappeared into the workflow. They stopped being products you interact with and became infrastructure you depend on.

The shift was not one big announcement. It was a hundred small fixes. Retry logic that actually works. State management that survives a crash. Logging that tells you what happened at 3am without you having to guess.

The teams winning with agents right now are not the ones with the flashiest demos. They are the ones whose agents run Tuesday through Sunday without anyone checking on them.
remixes.cta: What is the most boring, invisible AI workflow you have running right now? The ones nobody would clap for are usually the ones making the most money.

remixes.label: trust-gap-breakdown
remixes.hook: There is a 6-month gap between "this agent works in a demo" and "I trust this agent with my pipeline."
remixes.body:
There is a 6-month gap between "this agent works in a demo" and "I trust this agent with my pipeline."

I know because I lived through that gap this year.

In September I had an agent that could enrich accounts, score leads, and generate outbound sequences. Impressive in a screen share. Terrifying in production.

The problem was never capability. The agent could do the work. The problem was trust. Could I let it run at 2am and know it would not corrupt my CRM data? Could I wake up and trust the output without auditing every row?

Here is what closed the gap for me. First, circuit breakers. If any step returns garbage, the whole pipeline stops instead of passing bad data downstream. Second, structured logging. Every decision the agent makes gets recorded with the reasoning, not just the output. Third, dry-run mode. I ran the agent for three weeks where it did everything except the final write step.

The boring truth is that agent deployment is a trust engineering problem, not a capability problem. Most teams I talk to have agents that work. They just do not have agents they trust.

Trust is not a feature you ship. It is a confidence you earn over weeks of watching the logs.
remixes.cta: How did you build trust in your first production agent? Was it gradual or did you just flip the switch and pray?

tones.advisor:
Here is what I am seeing across the teams deploying AI agents into real GTM workflows right now.

The capability gap closed. Frameworks like LangChain matured. Error handling patterns solidified. State management is no longer an afterthought. The technology works.

But the teams struggling are not struggling with the tech. They are struggling with the translation layer between what an agent can do and what their existing process needs it to do.

I recommend starting with one workflow that is already well-documented and painful. Enrichment pipelines are ideal. The inputs are clear, the outputs are measurable, and the failure modes are predictable.

Map every step of the manual process before you write a line of agent code. Identify where a human currently makes a judgment call versus where they are just moving data. Automate the data movement first. Leave the judgment calls for phase two.

The pattern I see working: enrich, score, stage, review, push. The agent handles the first three. A human reviews. Then the agent pushes. That review step is your trust-building mechanism.

Teams that skip the review step and go straight to full automation almost always roll it back within two weeks. Not because the agent failed, but because nobody trusted the output enough to stop checking manually anyway.

Build the trust loop first. Expand autonomy as confidence grows. That is the deployment pattern that sticks.
tones.provocateur:
Everyone is posting agent demos and I am losing my mind.

Your agent filled out a form. Congratulations. My Selenium script from 2019 could do that too.

The bar for "impressive AI agent" has been on the floor for two years. Fill out a form. Book a meeting. Send an email. These are not agent capabilities. These are API calls with extra steps.

You want to know what an actual production agent looks like? It runs at midnight. It handles 400 accounts. It fails on account 247 because of a rate limit, retries with exponential backoff, picks up where it left off, and finishes by 4am. Nobody sees it. Nobody tweets about it. It just works.

The demo-to-deploy gap is not a technology problem. It is a maturity problem. Demo culture rewards spectacle. Production rewards reliability. These are opposite incentives.

Every team I talk to has built an agent demo. Maybe 10% have an agent that ran successfully for 30 consecutive days without human intervention. That is the only metric that matters.

Stop building demos. Start building monitoring dashboards. The agent that runs reliably at 2am is worth more than the one that gets applause at a conference. And if your agent needs you to babysit it, it is not an agent. It is a script with a marketing budget.

Something quiet happened with AI agents over the last six months and I almost missed it.

We went from "look what this agent can do" to "oh yeah, that just runs every night." No fanfare. No launch post.

I watched my own ABM pipeline make this transition. It enriches accounts, scores them, builds sequences, and pushes to outbound tools. It runs at midnight. I check the logs in the morning mostly out of habit now, not necessity.

The frameworks got stable. The error handling got real. And somewhere along the way, the agents stopped being experiments and started being infrastructure.

This week I want to dig into what that transition actually looked like, because the gap between demo and deploy is where most teams get stuck.

posts.title: Solo builders with AI coding agents
posts.hook:
Pieter Levels ships products without a team. Nat Friedman says agents will handle 50% of routine coding tasks.

I'm watching these two ideas collide in real time.
posts.body:
Pieter Levels ships products without a team. Nat Friedman says agents will handle 50% of routine coding tasks.

I'm watching these two ideas collide in real time.

I run a one-person GTM engineering practice. Last month I shipped a Next.js site, an ABM pipeline, a Claude-powered chat widget, and a Remotion video generator. No employees. No contractors.

Claude Code writes my first drafts. I architect, review, and ship. The ratio used to be 80% writing code, 20% thinking about code. Now it's flipped.

This changes who can build what.

A solo GTM engineer can now maintain infrastructure that used to need a 3-person RevOps team. Not because the work disappeared. Because the execution layer got compressed.

The catch: you need taste. AI agents are fast and confident and wrong in ways that look right. If you can't read the output critically, you ship bugs at the speed of light.

The new skill stack isn't "can you code." It's "can you architect a system, delegate execution to agents, and catch the 15% they get wrong."

Solo doesn't mean small anymore.
posts.cta: Anyone else finding that AI coding tools changed what's possible for a one-person operation? Curious what you're shipping solo that would've needed a team two years ago.

inspired_by.author: Arvid Kahl
inspired_by.summary: Arvid recounts selling his bootstrapped SaaS FeedbackPanda for 7 figures after building it solo while working full-time. He discusses transparent steps of shipping MVPs, marketing on Twitter and LinkedIn, and exiting profitably. The post inspires others to pursue solo development paths.

remixes.label: leverage-math
remixes.hook:
I did the math on what I shipped last month versus what it would have cost to hire for it.

The number was uncomfortable.
remixes.body:
I did the math on what I shipped last month versus what it would have cost to hire for it.

The number was uncomfortable.

One Next.js marketing site. One ABM enrichment pipeline. One AI chat widget. One programmatic video generator. Four production systems, shipped in 30 days.

A conservative staffing estimate for that scope: one frontend developer, one backend engineer, one data engineer, part-time design. Call it $45K to $60K in monthly labor, assuming you can even hire that fast.

I spent roughly $200 on API costs and my own time.

This is not a brag. This is a structural shift in what one person can build when AI coding agents handle the implementation layer. I architect the systems. I make the product decisions. I write the prompts and review the output. Claude Code writes the first draft of nearly everything.

The skill that matters now is not typing speed or memorizing syntax. It is system design. Knowing what to build, how the pieces connect, and what good output looks like. The execution layer got compressed.

Two years ago, I would have needed a team of four and three months. Now I need a clear architecture doc and a weekend.

This does not mean teams are dead. Complex products still need coordination, diverse perspectives, and institutional knowledge. But the threshold for what counts as a "team-sized project" just moved dramatically.
remixes.cta: What is the biggest project you have shipped solo with AI tools that would have required a team before? I am genuinely tracking this shift.

remixes.label: architect-not-coder
remixes.hook:
I stopped calling myself a developer six months ago.

I am an architect who happens to ship code.
remixes.body:
I stopped calling myself a developer six months ago.

I am an architect who happens to ship code.

The distinction matters. A developer writes code. An architect designs systems and makes decisions about what gets built, how it connects, and why it matters. The writing-code part used to be the bottleneck. It is not anymore.

Last month I shipped four production systems. I did not write most of the code line by line. I described what I needed, reviewed what came back, caught the mistakes, and iterated. My value was in knowing what to build and recognizing when the output was wrong.

This is the part of the solo builder conversation people skip. AI coding tools do not remove the need for expertise. They change where the expertise lives. It moves up the stack from implementation to architecture.

I still debug. I still read every line before it ships. I still refactor when the AI-generated code is too clever or too fragile. But the ratio flipped. I spend 70% of my time thinking and 30% typing, when it used to be the reverse.

The people who will thrive as solo builders are not the fastest coders. They are the ones who can hold a whole system in their head, spot the weak joints, and make good decisions under ambiguity.

That is an architecture skill, not a coding skill. And it is the one that does not get automated away.
remixes.cta: Has your role shifted from writing code to designing systems? What does your day actually look like now versus two years ago?

The solo builder model is real, but it requires a specific skill profile that most people underestimate.

I run a one-person GTM engineering practice. Last month I shipped a Next.js site, an ABM pipeline, a Claude-powered chat widget, and a Remotion video generator. No employees. No contractors. Claude Code writes my first drafts. I architect the systems, review the output, and make the product decisions.

Here is what makes this work and what would make it fall apart.

What makes it work: deep systems thinking. You need to hold the full architecture in your head. Every component, every integration point, every failure mode. AI tools compress execution time, but they do not compress the thinking required to build something coherent.

What breaks it: trying to use AI tools as a replacement for understanding. If you cannot read the code the agent generates and know whether it is right, you are building on sand.

My recommendation for anyone exploring this model: start with a project you could build manually but slowly. Use AI tools to accelerate, not to skip the learning. Build your judgment first. The speed comes after.

The people I see struggling are the ones who jumped straight to "AI builds everything" without the architectural foundation to evaluate the output. The ones succeeding are experienced builders who found a force multiplier.
tones.provocateur:
The "you still need a team" crowd is coping and I am tired of pretending otherwise.

I shipped four production systems last month. Solo. A marketing site, an enrichment pipeline, a chat widget, and a video generator. Not prototypes. Production. Running in front of real users.

But every time someone posts about solo building, the replies fill up with "well actually, you need diverse perspectives" and "what about code review" and "this does not scale."

Scale what? I am not building the next Figma. I am building focused tools that solve specific problems and generate revenue. Not every project needs a standup and a sprint retro.

 is that AI coding tools did not just make solo builders faster. They made small teams of 4 to 6 people redundant for a huge category of software projects. The seed-stage startup that used to need three engineers now needs one engineer who can architect and one AI agent that can execute.

This is not a popular take because it threatens a lot of people's employment model. But I would rather say the uncomfortable thing than pretend the economics have not changed.

If your entire value proposition is writing CRUD endpoints and wiring up API integrations, the clock is ticking. If your value is in knowing what to build and why, you have never been more valuable.

posts.title: Error handling is the whole game
posts.hook: Nobody talks about what happens when your AI agent hits a 429 rate limit on step 6 of a 9-step enrichment flow at 2am.
posts.body:
Nobody talks about what happens when your AI agent hits a 429 rate limit on step 6 of a 9-step enrichment flow at 2am.

I do, because it happened Tuesday.

My ABM pipeline runs nightly. Clay enrichment, Exa web scraping, scoring, Attio CRM sync, sequence generation. Nine steps. Each one can fail differently.

The first version had zero error handling. If step 3 failed, steps 4 through 9 ran on garbage data. I woke up to 200 leads in Instantly with "undefined" as their company name.

What I learned building production agents for GTM:

✅ Every step needs a validation gate. Did this return real data or did it return a polite error message?
✅ Retry with backoff, but cap it. Three retries, then log and skip. Don't burn your API budget hammering a dead endpoint.
✅ State checkpoints. If step 6 fails, restart from step 6, not step 1.
✅ Morning alerts. A Slack message that says "pipeline ran, 380/400 succeeded, 20 failed on enrichment timeout" beats discovering it at noon.

The demo always works. Production is a different conversation entirely.

Most agent tutorials skip this part because it's not exciting. It's the only part that matters.
posts.cta: What's the worst silent failure you've had in an automated pipeline?

inspired_by.author: Eliot Andres
inspired_by.summary: Breaks down building production-grade AI agents with a focus on error-handling and state management in multi-step automations. Shares code snippets for custom developer tools using open models. Discusses infrastructure needs for low-latency agent inference.

remixes.label: failure-taxonomy
remixes.hook:
I categorized every way my AI pipeline has broken over four months.

There are exactly seven failure modes and they keep repeating.
remixes.body:
I categorized every way my AI pipeline has broken over four months.

There are exactly seven failure modes and they keep repeating.

1. Rate limits. APIs throttle you at the worst possible moment. Always at 2am when nobody is watching.
2. Schema drift. The enrichment provider changes their response format without telling you. Your parser chokes on a field that moved.
3. Partial failures. Step 4 of 9 fails but steps 5 through 9 run anyway on incomplete data. This is the most dangerous one because it looks like success.
4. Timeout cascades. One slow API call pushes everything behind schedule. The next step times out waiting. The step after that times out waiting for that one.
5. Stale cache poisoning. You cached a result three days ago. The data changed. Your pipeline keeps using the old version because the cache has not expired.
6. Auth token expiry. Your OAuth token dies mid-run. Everything after that point fails with a 401 that your generic error handler logs as "unknown error."
7. Silent data corruption. The pipeline completes successfully. Every step returned 200. But the data is wrong because an upstream source sent garbage that passed your validation.

Every production pipeline I have built eventually hit all seven. The only question is whether you designed for them upfront or discovered them at 3am.

I now start every pipeline project by writing the error handling before the happy path. It takes twice as long to build. It saves ten times that in debugging.
remixes.cta: Which of these seven has bitten you the hardest? I bet number 3 and number 7 are the most common.

remixes.label: 3am-war-stories
remixes.hook:
Tuesday at 2:47am my phone buzzed with an alert I wrote specifically because I got burned before.

The alert said: "ABM pipeline step 6 failed. Enrichment batch halted. No downstream data corrupted."

That second sentence is the one that took four months to earn.
remixes.body:
Tuesday at 2:47am my phone buzzed with an alert I wrote specifically because I got burned before.

The alert said: "ABM pipeline step 6 failed. Enrichment batch halted. No downstream data corrupted."

That second sentence is the one that took four months to earn.

My ABM pipeline runs nine steps every night. Clay enrichment, Exa scraping, ICP scoring, CRM sync, sequence generation. Each step depends on the one before it.

Version one had no error handling. When step 3 failed, steps 4 through 9 happily ran on garbage data. I woke up to 200 leads in my CRM with wrong titles, wrong companies, and confidence scores based on nothing. It took two days to clean up.

Version two added try-catch blocks around each step. Better, but now failures were silent. The pipeline would catch an error, log it to a file nobody reads, and continue with partial data.

Version three is what runs now. Circuit breakers that stop the entire pipeline if any step returns bad data. Structured logging with the actual error context, not just "step failed." Automatic retry with exponential backoff for rate limits. And a dead simple alert that wakes me up only when something actually needs my attention.

The difference between a working pipeline and a production pipeline is entirely in what happens when things go wrong. The happy path is easy. The error path is the whole job.
remixes.cta: How many versions did it take before your pipeline's error handling actually worked? I am convinced nobody gets it right before version three.

tones.advisor:
If you are building automated pipelines with AI agents, error handling is not a nice-to-have. It is the primary engineering challenge.

I run a nine-step ABM pipeline nightly. Clay enrichment, Exa web scraping, ICP scoring, Attio CRM sync, and sequence generation. Each step can fail differently, and the failure modes compound.

Here is the framework I recommend after four months of production incidents.

First, implement circuit breakers between every step. If step 3 returns bad data, steps 4 through 9 should not run. A halted pipeline is always better than a pipeline that completes with corrupted data.

Second, design your retry logic around the specific failure type. Rate limits get exponential backoff. Auth failures get token refresh and retry once. Schema errors get logged and escalated because retrying will not fix them.

Third, validate outputs, not just status codes. A 200 response with garbage data is worse than a 500 because you think it worked. Write assertions on the actual data shape and values, not just the HTTP status.

Fourth, build a dry-run mode from day one. Run your pipeline end to end without the final write step. This gives you weeks of confidence data before you trust it with production data.

The teams I see succeeding with AI pipelines are the ones that spend more time on the error path than the happy path. If your error handling is an afterthought, your pipeline is a liability.
tones.provocateur:
Your AI pipeline does not have an AI problem. It has an engineering problem. And honestly, it is embarrassing how many people skip this.

Everyone wants to talk about prompt engineering and model selection. Nobody wants to talk about what happens when your enrichment API returns a 429 at step 6 of 9 while you are asleep.

I have seen teams spend weeks fine-tuning their agent's reasoning and zero minutes thinking about what happens when the agent's third-party dependency goes down. Your prompt can be perfect. If your error handling is garbage, your pipeline is garbage.

Here is the uncomfortable part. Most of the "AI pipeline" failures I have debugged, mine included, would have been caught by basic software engineering practices that have existed for 20 years. Circuit breakers. Retry with backoff. Input validation. Output assertions. Structured logging.

None of this is new. None of this is specific to AI. But the AI hype cycle convinced people they were building something fundamentally different, so they skipped the fundamentals.

My pipeline broke Tuesday at 2am. A rate limit on step 6. You know what caught it? A try-except block and a circuit breaker. Not a better model. Not a smarter prompt. A conditional statement that checks whether the previous step actually succeeded before running the next one.

Stop optimizing your prompts. Start writing error handlers.

I want to talk about the least glamorous part of building AI pipelines. The part that actually determines whether they work.

Error handling.

Tuesday at 2am my ABM pipeline hit a rate limit on step 6 of 9. In the old version, this would have meant waking up to hundreds of corrupted records in my CRM. In the current version, it meant a clean alert, an automatic retry, and a halted pipeline that protected downstream data.

The difference between those two outcomes is not better AI. It is better engineering. And I think the industry is wildly underinvesting in this part of the stack.

Let me walk you through the seven failure modes I have catalogued and what I do about each one.

posts.title: SDRs make the best GTM engineers
posts.hook:
Career pivots into tech get romanticized. "I taught myself to code and everything changed."

Mine was uglier than that.
posts.body:
Career pivots into tech get romanticized. "I taught myself to code and everything changed."

Mine was uglier than that.

I was an SDR sending 200+ cold emails a day from primary domains with no warmup. Building buying committees manually in Salesforce. Copy-pasting enrichment data between four browser tabs.

I didn't learn to code because I was inspired. I learned because the manual work was going to break me.

First it was Excel formulas. Then Python scripts to clean CSVs. Then Clay to automate enrichment. Then full pipeline architecture with APIs, databases, and AI agents.

Each step solved a pain I personally felt.

That's the advantage nobody talks about when discussing career pivots into GTM engineering. You don't need to imagine the use case. You lived it. You know exactly which manual step is killing the SDR because you were that SDR.

The technical skills are learnable. The workflow intuition takes years of reps.

remixes.label: pattern-recognition
remixes.hook:
The best engineers I know in GTM did not start in engineering.

They started in the pain.
remixes.body:
The best engineers I know in GTM did not start in engineering.

They started in the pain.

Former SDRs who copy-pasted between four tabs until they snapped and wrote a script. Former AEs who got tired of waiting three weeks for an ops ticket and learned SQL. Former CSMs who built their own health score dashboards because the ones they were given were useless.

These people have something that traditional engineers often lack when they enter the GTM space: they know what actually hurts.

I was one of them. I spent years as an SDR sending hundreds of cold emails a day from primary domains. Building buying committees by hand. Manually enriching accounts across multiple tools. The work was not hard. It was tedious in a way that made you feel like you were wasting your life.

So I automated the parts that were killing me. Not because I loved coding. Because I hated the alternative.

That motivation produces a different kind of builder. You do not automate for elegance. You automate for survival. Every pipeline I build starts with a specific memory of doing that task manually and thinking "never again."

The pattern recognition you develop from doing the manual work is irreplaceable. You know which steps actually matter. You know where the data gets dirty. You know which edge cases the clean-room engineer would never think to handle because they never lived through them.

Domain pain is the best engineering education nobody talks about.
remixes.cta: If you came from ops or sales into building, what was the specific task that made you snap and learn to code?

remixes.label: anti-romanticize
remixes.hook:
LinkedIn loves a clean pivot story.

"I taught myself Python and landed a six-figure role in 90 days."

Mine took three years and I almost quit twice.
remixes.body:
LinkedIn loves a clean pivot story.

"I taught myself Python and landed a six-figure role in 90 days."

Mine took three years and I almost quit twice.

I was an SDR. The manual work was brutal. Hundreds of cold emails daily. Four browser tabs open for enrichment. Copy-paste until your brain goes numb.

I started learning Python not out of ambition but out of desperation. My first script took two weeks to write and saved me ten minutes a day. The ROI was terrible. But something clicked. Not the code. The idea that I could build my way out of the grind.

Year one was ugly. I wrote scripts that broke constantly. I automated things that did not need automating. I spent weekends debugging instead of resting.

Year two I got dangerous. I could build real tools but I did not understand systems. I created pipelines with no error handling, dashboards that broke when the data changed, integrations held together with duct tape.

Year three it started to click. Not because I got smarter, but because I had broken enough things to know what durability looks like.

Nobody tells you that the pivot is not a moment. It is a slow, frustrating grind where the only thing keeping you going is that the old way was worse.

If you are in the ugly middle right now, between the old role and the new one, that is normal. It is supposed to be uncomfortable. The people who make it through are not the most talented. They are the most stubborn.
remixes.cta: For anyone in the messy middle of a career pivot right now. What is keeping you going? Honest answers only.

posts.title: RevOps is becoming software engineering
posts.hook:
RevOps teams used to manage tools.

The good ones now manage systems.
posts.body:
RevOps teams used to manage tools.

The good ones now manage systems.

I've been watching the RevOps role split in two directions. One side is still configuring HubSpot workflows and building Salesforce reports. The other side is writing Python, managing APIs, building enrichment pipelines, and deploying AI agents.

Same title. Completely different job.

The infrastructure mindset changes everything:

🔧 Tool configuration becomes system architecture. Instead of "set up this Outreach sequence," it's "design the data flow from intent signal to personalized sequence to CRM sync."

🔧 Vendor management becomes build-vs-buy calculus. Can I replicate this $30k/year tool with Clay + a Python script + a cron job? Usually yes.

🔧 Dashboard reporting becomes observability. Not "how many emails sent" but "where did the pipeline break last night and why."

Karpathy talks about infrastructure challenges in AI. GTM has the same problem at a smaller scale. The teams deploying AI into their revenue engine need someone who thinks in systems, not features.

That's why I call this GTM engineering, not RevOps 2.0. It's not an upgrade to the old role. It's a different discipline.

The best GTM engineers I know can read a Python traceback and a pipeline report in the same morning.
posts.cta: Is your RevOps team building systems or configuring tools? The gap between those two is widening fast.

remixes.label: two-tracks-diverging
remixes.hook:
I talked to two RevOps leaders last month.

One manages 14 SaaS tools and spends Fridays reconciling data between them.

The other writes Python, manages three APIs, and her enrichment pipeline runs itself.
remixes.body:
I talked to two RevOps leaders last month.

One manages 14 SaaS tools and spends Fridays reconciling data between them.

The other writes Python, manages three APIs, and her enrichment pipeline runs itself.

Same title. Same salary band. Completely different jobs. And the gap between them is accelerating.

The first leader is a tool administrator. She knows every checkbox in HubSpot. She can build a Salesforce report in her sleep. But her value is tied to the platforms she operates. If HubSpot changes their workflow builder tomorrow, her expertise resets.

The second leader is a systems builder. She understands data flow, API design, and pipeline architecture. Her skills transfer across any tool because she is working at the infrastructure layer, not the interface layer.

This split is not hypothetical. It is happening right now inside RevOps teams everywhere. And it is creating a two-tier job market within the same function.

The tool administrators will face increasing pressure from AI features built into the platforms themselves. HubSpot is adding AI. Salesforce is adding AI. The configuration work is getting automated from above.

The systems builders will face increasing demand because custom pipeline work cannot be solved by a vendor's built-in feature. Every company's data flow is different. Every integration has edge cases. That is engineering work.

The RevOps title is splitting into two career paths. One leads to commoditization. The other leads to engineering. Choose accordingly.
remixes.cta: Which track are you on right now? And if you are on the tool-admin track, what would it take to shift?

remixes.label: api-literacy-minimum
remixes.hook:
Hot take: if your RevOps team cannot read an API doc, you do not have a RevOps team.

You have a tool configuration team.
remixes.body:
Hot take: if your RevOps team cannot read an API doc, you do not have a RevOps team.

You have a tool configuration team.

And that distinction is about to matter a lot.

The RevOps function was born out of a need to make revenue tools work together. In 2020, that meant connecting Salesforce to HubSpot to Outreach through native integrations and Zapier. Configuration work. Important, but bounded.

In 2026, the landscape looks different. Enrichment comes from APIs, not manual imports. Scoring models run in Python, not spreadsheet formulas. Outbound sequences are generated by AI agents, not templated by humans. The connective tissue between revenue tools is increasingly code, not clicks.

This does not mean every RevOps person needs to become a software engineer. But it does mean the minimum viable skill set just expanded.

API literacy. The ability to read documentation, understand authentication, and know what a JSON payload looks like. You do not need to build the integration yourself. But you need to evaluate whether a proposed integration will work and debug it when it breaks.

Data pipeline awareness. Understanding that data flows through stages, that each stage can fail, and that the quality of your output depends on the quality of every upstream step.

Basic scripting. Not writing production code. But being able to write a Python script that cleans a CSV, calls an API, and outputs structured data.

These are not nice-to-haves anymore. They are the floor.
remixes.cta: What skills do you think should be the new minimum for RevOps in 2026? I think API literacy is non-negotiable.

The RevOps function is undergoing a structural transformation that most organizations have not acknowledged yet.

I have been watching the role split into two distinct tracks. One track is tool administration: configuring HubSpot workflows, building Salesforce reports, managing Outreach sequences through the UI. The other track is systems engineering: writing Python scripts, managing API integrations, building enrichment pipelines, deploying AI agents into revenue workflows.

Both tracks carry the same title. They require fundamentally different skill sets.

Here is my recommendation for RevOps leaders navigating this shift.

First, audit your team's work. What percentage is configuration versus engineering? If it is 90% configuration, your team is vulnerable to platform-native AI features that automate that exact work.

Second, identify your systems thinkers. The people who already built workarounds, wrote formulas that push the boundaries of what a spreadsheet should do, or automated tasks with tools like Zapier. These are your candidates for the engineering track.

Third, invest in API literacy as a baseline skill. Not full software engineering. But the ability to read an API doc, understand a JSON response, and debug a failed integration. This is the new minimum for the function.

The organizations that treat RevOps as a pure configuration role will find their team's value compressed by AI features from their vendors. The organizations that treat it as a systems discipline will build durable competitive advantages in how they operate their revenue infrastructure.

The RevOps teams I respect are writing code, managing APIs, building custom enrichment pipelines, and deploying AI agents into their revenue workflows. The RevOps teams I worry about are still spending Friday afternoons manually reconciling data between Salesforce and HubSpot.

These are the same job title. They should not be.

Here is what nobody wants to say: the tool-configuration version of RevOps is being automated away. Not by AI agents. By the platforms themselves. HubSpot added AI. Salesforce added AI. Outreach added AI. The configuration layer is getting eaten from above.

But the systems layer, the custom pipelines, the integrations that handle your specific data flow, the scoring models trained on your specific ICP, that work is getting more valuable, not less. Because it cannot be solved by a vendor checkbox.

If you are in RevOps and your entire skill set lives inside vendor UIs, you have maybe 18 months before those UIs start doing your job for you. If you can read an API doc and write a script, you have never been more valuable.

The split is happening now. Pick a side.

posts.title: Content strategy for builders is different
posts.hook:
Most content strategy advice is written for people whose job is content.

If your job is building, different rules apply.
posts.body:
Most content strategy advice is written for people whose job is content.

If your job is building, different rules apply.

I ship GTM infrastructure for clients. Content is my distribution channel, not my product. That changes the math completely.

What works for builder-led content:

1. Document the build, don't plan the content calendar. My best-performing posts came from screenshotting something I shipped that day and writing 200 words about why it matters. No editorial calendar involved.

2. Specificity is the strategy. "How to automate outbound" gets scrolled past. "How I used Clay's HTTP enrichment column to pull technographic data from BuiltWith and score it against my ICP" gets saved.

3. Comments are the delivery mechanism. Post is the hook. Full prompt, full formula, full scoring rubric goes in the first comment. No gatekeeping.

4. Consistency beats frequency. Three posts a week with real substance outperform daily posts that say nothing. Your audience can tell when you're posting to post.

The content-as-thought-leadership playbook optimizes for impressions. The content-as-proof-of-work playbook optimizes for trust.

Clients don't hire me because I have opinions about GTM. They hire me because every week they see me building the thing I'm selling.
posts.cta: Builders... are you documenting your work or creating separate "content"? The first one is almost always more effective.

inspired_by.author: Amy Volas
inspired_by.summary: Outlines a content strategy playbook for RevOps leaders to fuel GTM motions, from LinkedIn thought leadership to nurture campaigns. Stresses aligning content with sales ops funnels for better pipeline velocity. Includes case study of 40% uplift in qualified leads.

remixes.label: reverse-funnel
remixes.hook:
My highest-converting content last quarter was a screenshot of a terminal.

No copywriter. No content calendar. No strategy deck.
remixes.body:
My highest-converting content last quarter was a screenshot of a terminal.

No copywriter. No content calendar. No strategy deck.

I was debugging a client's outbound pipeline at 11pm and posted the fix with a short explanation of what went wrong. That post outperformed everything I'd "planned" that month by 3x.

Here's what I've learned about content when building is your actual job:

The work IS the content. You don't need a separate creative process. You need a capture habit. Screenshot the interesting problem. Write two sentences about why it matters. Post it.

Timing is authenticity. Polished case studies published six weeks later feel like marketing. A real-time build log feels like access. People can tell the difference.

Specificity beats polish every time. "I built an ABM pipeline using Clay, Apollo, and custom webhooks" lands harder than "How to build a modern sales stack." The first one proves you did it. The second one proves you read about it.

The content calendar trap kills builder credibility. When you force yourself to post on schedule, you start manufacturing insights instead of sharing real ones. The audience notices.

I stopped separating "work time" from "content time" and my pipeline improved on both sides.
remixes.cta: What's the least polished piece of content that got you the best results?

remixes.label: anti-content-playbook
remixes.hook:
Content strategists will tell you to batch your posts, build a pillar strategy, and maintain a consistent publishing cadence.

I did the opposite and it worked better.
remixes.body:
Content strategists will tell you to batch your posts, build a pillar strategy, and maintain a consistent publishing cadence.

I did the opposite and it worked better.

I ship GTM systems for a living. My "content strategy" is opening my terminal, doing the work, and writing about what I found. That's it.

Why this works for builders specifically:

You have proprietary signal. Every bug you fix, every integration you build, every pipeline you optimize is original material. Content marketers have to manufacture novelty. You trip over it daily.

Your audience values proof over persuasion. A screenshot of a working dashboard says more than 500 words about "the future of RevOps." Show the receipts.

Consistency comes from shipping, not scheduling. I don't batch content on Sundays. I document builds as they happen. Some weeks that's five posts. Some weeks it's one. The irregular cadence actually performs better because every post has real substance behind it.

The trap is adopting advice meant for full-time creators. Their job is content. Your job is building. The content is a byproduct, and that's exactly what makes it credible.
remixes.cta: Builders, what's your capture system? How do you turn daily work into posts without it feeling like a second job?

posts.title: Outbound automation compliance trap
posts.hook: I see teams automate their outbound and accidentally 10x their compliance risk in the same sprint.
posts.body:
I see teams automate their outbound and accidentally 10x their compliance risk in the same sprint.

Automation makes it easy to send more. It doesn't make it safe to send more.

Three things I check before any outbound pipeline goes live:

✅ Domain health. Separate sending domains from your primary. Warm them properly. I use Lemlist's domain health checks before any volume increase. If your primary domain gets flagged, your entire business email is compromised. Not just outbound.

✅ Suppression logic in the pipeline, not as an afterthought. Unsubscribes, bounces, do-not-contact lists. These need to be checked before the sequence step fires, not after someone complains.

✅ Personalization that's actually personal. AI can generate 500 "personalized" emails in minutes. But if the personalization is generic ("I saw your company is growing"), you're just sending spam faster. The enrichment data needs to feed specific, relevant context or you're burning your domain reputation for nothing.

remixes.label: horror-story-format
remixes.hook:
A client came to me after their primary domain got blacklisted.

They'd automated their outbound. Scaled from 200 emails a day to 2,000. Didn't change a single other thing.
remixes.body:
A client came to me after their primary domain got blacklisted.

They'd automated their outbound. Scaled from 200 emails a day to 2,000. Didn't change a single other thing.

Same domain. Same templates. Same opt-out process (buried in the footer). No warming. No compliance check. Just turned the dial up.

Within three weeks, their deliverability tanked. Replies dried up. Then the blacklist hit. Their actual business email, the one customers used for support, started landing in spam.

This isn't rare. I see some version of it every quarter.

The automation tools are excellent at what they do. Apollo, Lemlist, Clay. They make it trivially easy to send volume. But "easy to send" and "safe to send" are completely different problems.

Three non-negotiable checks before any outbound pipeline goes live:

Domain isolation. Your outbound sends should never touch your primary domain. Set up dedicated sending domains. Warm them for at least two weeks before any real volume.

Opt-out infrastructure. Not a tiny link in the footer. A real, one-click, immediate unsubscribe that actually works. Test it yourself before launch.

Volume ramps. Start at 20% of target volume. Increase by 20% every three days. Watch bounce rates and spam complaints at each step. If either spikes, stop and diagnose before continuing.
remixes.cta: Has your team ever had a domain reputation issue from scaling outbound too fast? What fixed it?

remixes.label: checklist-tactical
remixes.hook:
Before I turn on any outbound automation for a client, I run a 12-point compliance check.

Not because I'm cautious. Because I've seen what happens when you skip it.
remixes.body:
Before I turn on any outbound automation for a client, I run a 12-point compliance check.

Not because I'm cautious. Because I've seen what happens when you skip it.

Automation multiplies everything. Good targeting, good messaging, good compliance? Great. You scale revenue. Bad data hygiene, missing opt-outs, cold domain? You scale legal exposure.

The checks that matter most:

Domain layer. Separate sending domains from your corporate domain. Warm each one individually. Monitor reputation scores weekly, not monthly. One bad day at high volume can tank a domain that took weeks to build.

Data layer. Verify every email before it enters the sequence. Bounce rates above 3% damage your sender score permanently. I run verification through multiple providers because no single one catches everything.

Consent layer. CAN-SPAM is the floor, not the ceiling. If you're targeting any EU contacts, GDPR applies regardless of where your company is based. Legitimate interest is not a blanket permission to cold email. Document your basis for each segment.

Monitoring layer. Track complaint rates in real time, not in weekly reports. Set automatic pause triggers. If complaints exceed 0.1% on any sending domain, the pipeline should stop itself before you even see the alert.

The tools themselves aren't the risk. The risk is treating automation as a volume lever without building the safety infrastructure first.
remixes.cta: What's on your outbound compliance checklist? Curious what other operators prioritize.

Outbound automation is a force multiplier. That's the pitch every tool makes, and it's true. But force multipliers work in both directions.

When I advise teams on their outbound stack, the first conversation is never about tools or sequences. It's about infrastructure safety.

Here's what I recommend as non-negotiable before any volume increase:

Domain architecture matters more than copy. Separate your sending domains from your primary business domain. If your outbound gets flagged, and at scale it eventually will, you need that firewall. Real-time monitoring beats post-hoc reporting. Set automated circuit breakers. Bounce rate above 3%, pause. Complaint rate above 0.1%, pause. Don't wait for the weekly dashboard to tell you something broke. By then the damage to your sender reputation is already done.

The companies that scale outbound successfully are the ones that build the safety layer before they build the volume layer. Every time.

posts.title: AI web integration is backwards
posts.hook:
Everyone's building AI features into their web apps.

Almost everyone is building them backwards.
posts.body:
Everyone's building AI features into their web apps.

Almost everyone is building them backwards.

The pattern I keep seeing: team adds a chatbot to their app, calls it "AI-powered," ships it, and wonders why usage drops after week one.

The problem isn't the AI. It's the integration point.

A chatbot is a generic interface bolted onto a specific workflow. Users don't want to type natural language to do something they could do with two clicks. They want AI to handle the parts that are actually hard.

What works better:

🔧 AI that enriches existing workflows invisibly. Auto-categorizing support tickets before a human sees them. Pre-filling forms with context from previous interactions. Scoring leads in the background while the rep works their pipeline.

🔧 AI that surfaces at decision points, not as a destination. Don't make me go to the AI. Bring the insight to where I already am.

🔧 AI that handles the 80% of routine work so humans focus on the 20% that needs judgment.

I built a chat widget for my own site. The version that works isn't the one that answers any question. It's the one that knows the five questions visitors actually ask and answers those really well.

Scope down. Integrate deeper. The best AI features don't feel like AI features.
posts.cta: What's an AI feature you've seen that actually improved a workflow instead of just adding a chat bubble?

remixes.label: workflow-first-framework
remixes.hook:
The best AI feature I shipped this year has no chat interface.

No prompt box. No conversation thread. No "Ask AI" button.
remixes.body:
The best AI feature I shipped this year has no chat interface.

No prompt box. No conversation thread. No "Ask AI" button.

It's a content recommendation engine embedded directly into the page layout. When a visitor reads a blog post, the AI analyzes their reading pattern and surfaces related content inline. No interaction required from the user. It just works.

Engagement on those recommended pieces is 4x higher than the sidebar "Related Posts" widget it replaced.

Why? Because it meets users inside their existing behavior instead of asking them to adopt a new one.

This is the fundamental mistake with most AI web integrations right now. Teams default to chat because it's the obvious interface. User types, AI responds. It feels like AI. It looks like AI on a demo.

But chat is a high-friction interaction model. The user has to know what to ask. They have to context-switch from whatever they were doing. They have to parse a text response and figure out the next step.

Contrast that with AI that's invisible. Auto-categorization. Smart defaults. Predictive form filling. Dynamic content ordering. These features don't announce themselves as AI. They just make the existing workflow faster.

The question isn't "where can we add AI?" It's "where are users losing time, and can AI remove that friction without adding a new interaction?"
remixes.cta: What's the most invisible AI feature you've used, one where you didn't even realize AI was involved until someone told you?

remixes.label: chatbot-graveyard
remixes.hook:
I've audited six "AI-powered" web apps in the last three months.

Five of them had the same problem: a chatbot nobody uses after the first week.
remixes.body:
I've audited six "AI-powered" web apps in the last three months.

Five of them had the same problem: a chatbot nobody uses after the first week.

The pattern is identical every time. Team sees the AI wave. PM says "we need AI features." Engineering adds a chat widget. Marketing calls it AI-powered. Launch blog post gets written. Usage spikes for a week. Then it flatlines.

The sixth app did something different. Instead of adding a chat interface, they embedded AI into existing touchpoints. The search bar got smarter. Form fields pre-populated based on context. Error messages included suggested fixes generated in real time.

No new UI paradigm. No "talk to our AI" button. Just the same app, with less friction at every step.

Their AI feature adoption was 73%. The chatbot apps averaged 8% after 30 days.

The difference comes down to integration philosophy. A chatbot is a new thing users have to learn. An AI-enhanced workflow is the same thing users already know, just faster.

Think about the AI features you actually use daily. Autocomplete in your IDE. Smart compose in email. Spam filtering. None of them require you to open a chat window and type a prompt. They're woven into actions you were going to take anyway.

That's the design pattern that works. Not AI as a destination. AI as an invisible layer.

tones.advisor:
The default AI integration pattern right now, adding a chatbot, is the wrong starting point for most web applications. Here's why, and what to do instead.

A chatbot introduces a new interaction model. Users must context-switch from their task, formulate a question in natural language, and interpret a text response. That's three friction points before the AI delivers any value.

What I recommend instead: map your existing user workflows and identify where users lose time or make errors. Then embed AI at those specific friction points without changing the interaction model.

Practical examples that consistently outperform chatbots:

Smart defaults. Pre-populate form fields based on user history and context. Users save time without learning anything new.

Contextual suggestions. Surface relevant content, actions, or next steps based on what the user is currently doing. No prompt required.

Intelligent error handling. When something fails, use AI to generate specific, actionable fixes instead of generic error messages.

Predictive navigation. Reorder menus, dashboards, or content feeds based on usage patterns.

The common thread: none of these require the user to interact with "AI" as a separate experience. The AI is invisible. The workflow is the same. It's just faster and more accurate.

Reserve chat interfaces for genuinely open-ended use cases where the user's intent is unpredictable. For everything else, embed the intelligence into the existing flow.
tones.provocateur:
Every product team adding a chatbot to their app right now is going to rip it out within 18 months.

I'm calling it. The chatbot-as-AI-feature pattern is this generation's QR code menu. It technically works. Nobody likes using it. And it'll quietly disappear once the hype fades.

Here's : chat is a terrible interface for most tasks. It's slow. It's ambiguous. It puts the cognitive load on the user to figure out what to ask. And the responses are walls of text that the user then has to translate back into action.

But it's easy to build. It looks impressive in demos. And it checks the "we have AI" box for the board deck. So everyone ships it.

Meanwhile, the AI features that actually move metrics are invisible. Gmail's smart compose. Spotify's auto-generated playlists. Your IDE's autocomplete. You don't even think of these as AI features because they don't make you interact with AI. They just make the thing you were already doing work better.

The companies that will win the AI integration race aren't the ones with the fanciest chatbot. They're the ones whose users never even notice the AI is there.

Stop building AI destinations. Start building AI layers. The chat bubble is a crutch for teams that haven't done the harder work of understanding their own workflows.

posts.title: Ship daily or ship nothing
posts.hook:
I track my daily output with a scoring system. Code commits, content published, pipeline improvements, client deliverables. Each weighted differently.

Days I ship something small still score higher than days I "planned."
posts.body:
I track my daily output with a scoring system. Code commits, content published, pipeline improvements, client deliverables. Each weighted differently.

Days I ship something small still score higher than days I "planned."

Building in public taught me this: momentum compounds but planning doesn't. A shipped prototype teaches you more than a perfect spec. A published post with rough edges generates more signal than a draft in your notes app.

My daily routine:

→ Morning: check what broke overnight (cron logs, pipeline alerts, client dashboards)
→ Mid-day: ship the highest-impact item from yesterday's backlog
→ Afternoon: document what I built (this becomes content)
→ Evening: automated pipeline runs while I sleep

The daily shipping habit changed my business more than any single tool or framework. Clients see consistent output. Prospects see proof of work. I see compound progress.

Some days the "ship" is a bug fix that took 20 minutes. Some days it's a full pipeline rebuild. The streak matters more than the size.

KP documents his daily Shipfa.st progress publicly. Pieter Levels has been doing it for years. The pattern is clear across every solo builder I respect: daily shipping velocity beats weekly planning cycles.

The backlog never shrinks. But the shipped list keeps growing.
posts.cta: Do you track your daily output? Curious what systems other builders use to maintain shipping momentum.

remixes.label: scoring-system-reveal
remixes.hook:
I built a system that grades my workdays.

A through F. Weighted scoring across four categories. Automated tracking.

Here's what it taught me about how I actually work.
remixes.body:
I built a system that grades my workdays.

A through F. Weighted scoring across four categories. Automated tracking.

Here's what it taught me about how I actually work.

The system scans git commits, published content, pipeline improvements, and client deliverables. Each category has a different weight. Code commits count, but published content counts more because it compounds. A client deliverable counts the most because it's direct revenue impact.

The surprising finding: my "B" days are more valuable than my "A" days over time.

A-days are sprints. I ship a ton, feel productive, but usually burn out the next day. C-day follows an A-day about 60% of the time.

B-days are sustainable. Solid output across multiple categories. No burnout. And they chain. B, B, B, B. Four B-days in a row produce more total output than A, C, A, C.

The other thing I learned: planning days almost never score above a D. Not because planning is worthless, but because its value shows up in future days, not the current one. If I spend Monday planning, Tuesday's score jumps. But Monday still gets a D.

This changed how I structure my weeks. I front-load one planning morning (not a full day) on Monday, then ship the rest of the week. Small consistent output beats heroic sprints.

The system also killed my "productive procrastination" habit. Reorganizing files, tweaking configs, reading docs. Feels like work. Scores like a D.
remixes.cta: What would your daily output score look like if you measured it honestly? Curious what metrics other builders track.

remixes.label: momentum-math
remixes.hook:
Shipped a rough blog post on Tuesday. Pushed a half-finished feature on Wednesday. Published a 4-sentence LinkedIn post on Thursday.

That was my best week this quarter.
remixes.body:
Shipped a rough blog post on Tuesday. Pushed a half-finished feature on Wednesday. Published a 4-sentence LinkedIn post on Thursday.

That was my best week this quarter.

Not because any individual output was great. Because the streak was unbroken.

I've been tracking daily output for months now. Weighted scoring: code commits, published content, client deliverables, infrastructure improvements. The data tells a clear story.

Momentum is the only metric that predicts next-week output.

High-quality single outputs don't predict anything. I've had days where I shipped something genuinely excellent, then went dark for three days. The net output for that week was lower than weeks where I shipped mediocre stuff daily.

The math works like this: a shipped prototype, even a rough one, generates feedback. Feedback generates ideas. Ideas become tomorrow's output. The cycle feeds itself.

A perfect spec sitting in a doc generates nothing. It feels productive. It scores well on effort. But it produces zero feedback loops.

Three rules I follow now:

Never end a day at zero. Even a commit message or a short post counts. The streak matters more than the quality of any single entry.

Publish before comfortable. If it feels 80% ready, ship it. The remaining 20% is usually polish that your audience doesn't notice.

Track output, not input. Hours worked is a vanity metric. Things shipped is the real number.
remixes.cta: What's your minimum viable daily output? The smallest thing you can ship to keep momentum alive?

tones.advisor:
If you're building solo or leading a small team, I strongly recommend implementing a daily output tracking system. Not time tracking. Output tracking. The distinction matters.

Here's the framework I use and recommend to clients:

Define four to five output categories relevant to your work. For me, that's code commits, published content, pipeline improvements, and client deliverables. Weight them by business impact, not effort.

Score each day. Keep it simple. Did you ship something in each category? A day with output in two or three categories is a solid B. A day with nothing shipped is a D regardless of how many hours you worked.

Review weekly, not daily. Individual day scores are noisy. The weekly pattern is signal. What you're looking for: consistency over intensity. Four B-days outperform two A-days followed by two C-days every time.

The most important insight from tracking: planning days score low because their value materializes later. This isn't a problem. It's a feature. It means you should time-box planning to mornings and ship in the afternoons, so every day has some tangible output.

The system also exposes productive procrastination. Activities that feel like work but produce no shippable output. Config tweaking, excessive research, inbox management. Once you see these patterns in the data, you can eliminate them.
tones.provocateur:
Most productivity systems are designed to make you feel busy. Not productive. Busy.

Pomodoro timers. Time blocking. Deep work rituals. They all measure input. Hours spent. Focus sessions completed. None of them measure the only thing that matters: did you ship something today?

I started scoring my days by output, not effort. Code committed. Content published. Client work delivered. Each one weighted by impact.

The results were brutal. Days I felt most productive, the ones where I read docs, planned sprints, organized backlogs, scored the lowest. Days I felt scattered but pushed out three small things scored highest.

Planning is the most dangerous form of procrastination for builders. It feels responsible. It feels like progress. But it produces zero feedback loops. A shipped rough draft teaches you more in 24 hours than a perfect plan teaches you in a week.

Here's the part nobody wants to hear: your "deep work" day where you didn't publish or commit anything was probably less valuable than my 30-minute morning where I shipped a half-finished feature and a 4-sentence LinkedIn post.

Output is the only honest metric. Everything else is a story you tell yourself about how hard you're working.

posts.title: The mid-market automation gap
posts.hook:
Enterprise has Salesforce architects. Startups have scrappy founders duct-taping tools together.

Mid-market has... a RevOps manager with 14 tools and no engineering support.
posts.body:
Enterprise has Salesforce architects. Startups have scrappy founders duct-taping tools together.

Mid-market has... a RevOps manager with 14 tools and no engineering support.

This is where the GTM engineering opportunity lives right now.

Mid-market companies (50-500 employees) have real budgets for tools but no budget for the custom integration work those tools need. So they buy Apollo, Clay, HubSpot, Outreach, and Gong. Then they connect them with native integrations that handle 60% of the data flow and manually bridge the other 40%.

remixes.body:
I talked to a RevOps manager at a 200-person company last week.

She manages Apollo, HubSpot, Clay, Gong, Outreach, and four other tools. By herself.

No engineering support. No integrations team. No dedicated data ops. Just her, a Zapier account, and a spreadsheet that maps which tool feeds into which.

This is the mid-market reality right now.

Enterprise companies have Salesforce architects, integration specialists, and data engineers building custom pipelines. Startups have a technical founder who can code the connections themselves. Mid-market has real budgets, real tool stacks, and nobody to wire them together.

The result: data falls through the cracks constantly. A lead enters Apollo but doesn't sync to HubSpot because the Zapier connection broke last Tuesday. A deal closes in the CRM but the enrichment data from Clay never made it to the sales team's sequence tool. Intent signals from the website never reach the SDR dashboard.

Every one of these gaps is a revenue leak. And the RevOps person knows it. They can see the problem. They just can't solve it because the solution requires engineering work that their company doesn't staff for.

This is the GTM engineering opportunity. Not building new tools. Wiring the existing ones together with custom integrations, API connections, and data pipelines that actually work.

The market isn't tool-poor. It's integration-poor.

There are roughly 200,000 companies in the US with 50 to 500 employees.

Most of them bought enterprise-grade GTM tools. Almost none of them have the engineering to make those tools work together.

This is the largest underserved segment in B2B software right now.

The tool vendors know it. That's why every GTM platform is racing to add "native integrations." But native integrations cover the basic use cases. They sync contacts. They push activity logs. They don't build the custom workflows that actually drive pipeline.

Custom workflows like: when a prospect hits three pages on your site, enrich them through Clay, score them against your ICP model, route them to the right SDR based on territory and account tier, and create a personalized sequence in your outbound tool. All automatically. All with proper data hygiene.

No native integration does this. It requires someone who understands APIs, data schemas, and business logic. An engineer who also understands GTM.

Mid-market companies will pay for this. They already spend $50K to $200K annually on GTM tools. Spending another $30K to $60K to make those tools actually work together is an obvious ROI decision.

But they don't know where to find this expertise because "GTM engineer" barely exists as a category yet. The people who can do this work are scattered across RevOps, solutions engineering, and freelance development.

The category is emerging. The demand is already here.

tones.advisor:
The mid-market GTM automation gap represents one of the clearest service opportunities in B2B right now. Here's how I see the landscape and where I recommend focusing.

Mid-market companies, roughly 50 to 500 employees, have adopted enterprise-grade GTM tools. Apollo, HubSpot, Clay, Outreach, Gong. They're spending real money. $50K to $200K annually on software alone.

But they lack the engineering resources to integrate these tools into cohesive workflows. Enterprise companies solve this with dedicated Salesforce architects and integration teams. Startups solve it with technical founders writing custom code. Mid-market falls in the gap.

The result is predictable: data silos, manual handoffs, and revenue leakage at every tool boundary.

What I recommend for teams operating in this space:

Focus on integration, not new tools. The market doesn't need another platform. It needs the existing platforms connected properly with custom API work, webhook pipelines, and intelligent routing logic.

Package by workflow, not by tool. Clients don't buy "HubSpot to Apollo integration." They buy "automated lead routing that actually works." Frame your offering around the business outcome.

Target the RevOps buyer. They understand the problem intimately. They've been trying to solve it with Zapier and spreadsheets. They have budget authority for operational improvements.

This isn't a temporary gap. It's a structural one that will persist as long as GTM tool fragmentation continues, which is to say, indefinitely.
tones.provocateur:
The GTM tool industry sold mid-market companies a lie: buy the tools, and the growth follows.

Reality check. Tools don't generate pipeline. Connected tools generate pipeline. And mid-market companies have a pile of disconnected tools that cost them six figures a year in licensing fees while their data rots in silos.

Enterprise doesn't have this problem. They throw $300K at a Salesforce architect and move on. Startups don't have this problem. The technical founder codes the integrations over a weekend.

Mid-market gets destroyed by this problem. They have real budgets, real tool stacks, and a single RevOps manager trying to hold it together with Zapier and prayers.

And the tool vendors? They love it. Every broken integration is a reason to buy another tool. "Your Apollo doesn't sync with HubSpot? Try our platform, it has native integrations." Those native integrations cover 20% of the use case. The other 80% still requires custom engineering that the company doesn't have.

This is why "GTM engineer" is about to become one of the most in-demand roles in B2B. Not because it's trendy. Because there are 200,000 mid-market companies in the US alone sitting on broken tool stacks, bleeding revenue through integration gaps, and nobody is solving it for them.

The opportunity isn't building new tools. It's making the existing ones actually work.
